title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: An international development organisation has asked you to prepare a report on the brain drain phenomenon affecting healthcare systems in lower-income countries. Your report should identify the scale of the problem, analyse the underlying causes, and recommend policy measures that could help retain skilled healthcare workers in their home countries.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Report on Brain Drain in Healthcare Systems in Lower-Income Countries**

**Introduction**
This report examines the brain drain phenomenon as it affects healthcare systems in lower-income countries, analyses the principal drivers of skilled emigration, and recommends evidence-based policy measures to improve retention. Findings draw on World Health Organisation workforce data, academic literature and case studies from three affected regions.

**Scale of the Problem**
The WHO estimates that fifty-seven countries face critical shortages of health workers. Many of these shortfalls are compounded by emigration to high-income countries. Ghana, for example, has trained several thousand doctors in the past two decades; the majority now practise abroad. This deficit directly contributes to preventable mortality and constrains the delivery of even basic healthcare services.

**Underlying Causes**
Three factors consistently drive emigration: salary differentials that can exceed tenfold between origin and destination countries; poor working conditions, including inadequate equipment, unsafe environments and insufficient professional development opportunities; and political instability or weak governance that reduces confidence in long-term career prospects.

**Recommendations**
Three interconnected measures are recommended. First, destination-country governments and health systems should establish mandatory financial compensation agreements with origin countries from which they recruit. Second, international donors should fund targeted salary supplementation schemes in critically understaffed specialisms. Third, regional cooperation between health ministries should create shared career development frameworks that improve professional prospects without requiring emigration.

**Conclusion**
Retaining skilled healthcare workers requires addressing systemic incentive structures, not simply appealing to individual workers' sense of national obligation.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt: You are a researcher at a university in a developing country. Several of your most talented colleagues have recently emigrated to positions in wealthy countries. Write a formal letter to your country's Minister for Education and Research expressing your concerns about the brain drain affecting your institution, identifying the factors driving emigration, and proposing measures the government could take to improve retention of skilled researchers.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Minister Achebe,

I write to you as a senior research fellow at Kumasi Technical University with deep concern about the accelerating departure of skilled academics from our institution and from higher education across the country more broadly.

In the past eighteen months alone, seven of our most productive researchers have accepted positions at universities in Europe and North America. I do not write to criticise their decisions: in each case, the financial differential was considerable, the research infrastructure offered was superior, and the professional development opportunities were, frankly, incomparable to what our university can currently provide. If I am honest, I have myself received two approaches from foreign institutions that I have found increasingly difficult to decline.

I am writing because I believe this trend, if unaddressed, will fundamentally undermine the long-term development of our national research capacity and, with it, the country's ability to train the next generation of scientists, engineers and medical professionals. The consequences extend far beyond the academic sector.

I would respectfully propose three areas for government action. An emergency salary review benchmarked against regional comparators would immediately reduce the financial incentive to emigrate. A dedicated research infrastructure fund, capitalised through public and private partnership, would improve the working conditions that researchers cite alongside salary as their primary dissatisfiers. Finally, diaspora engagement programmes that enable emigrant researchers to contribute to national projects remotely could rebuild connections that benefit both parties.

I remain available to provide further evidence or to discuss these proposals at your convenience.

Yours respectfully,
Dr Emmanuel Asante
Department of Applied Sciences, Kumasi Technical University

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: You have recently watched a documentary series called 'Leaving Home' that follows skilled workers from developing countries who have emigrated to Europe and North America, exploring their motivations, their experiences of adapting to a new country, and the complex feelings they hold about the communities they left behind. Write a review for a current affairs magazine evaluating the series' emotional depth, its handling of economic and political context, and whether it successfully humanises the brain drain debate.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Moving, Complex and Long Overdue: A Review of 'Leaving Home'**

The brain drain debate is frequently conducted in the language of statistics — emigration figures, workforce deficits, GDP impacts. 'Leaving Home', the six-part documentary series produced by Frontline Documentaries, makes the valuable and overdue intervention of placing human beings at its centre, and the result is television that is simultaneously affecting and illuminating.

The series follows seven individuals from Nigeria, the Philippines, Romania and Bolivia who have emigrated to work in healthcare, engineering and academia in the United Kingdom, Germany and Canada. Director Priya Nair gives each subject the time and space to articulate their motivations with nuance: the tensions between professional ambition and national loyalty, between the financial realities of supporting extended families and the guilt of contributing to their country's skills crisis, are rendered with remarkable honesty.

The series is equally strong on contextual framing. Each episode opens with a brief but precise explanation of the structural factors — salary differentials, working conditions, political instability — that drive emigration, ensuring that viewers do not reduce the subjects' decisions to individual choices made in a vacuum. The ethical implications of wealthy countries' active recruitment of skilled workers from under-resourced nations are raised clearly without tipping into polemic.

If the series has a limitation, it is that the perspectives of those who chose to stay — and the communities experiencing the consequences of departure — receive comparatively limited screen time.

Nevertheless, 'Leaving Home' is compelling, humane and genuinely important journalism. Strongly recommended.
